nativeRange.habitat: Found across the Indo-West Pacific in reef-associated environments, typically in current-prone channels with rich soft-bottom habitats and on rocky or coralline substrate near algae, at depths of roughly 10 to 300 m.

diet.notes: Ambush predator that has been reported to prefer live foods such as feeder shrimp, ghost shrimp, and small fish. Many specimens are reluctant to accept frozen fare, though some can be trained onto frozen silversides or shrimp using tongs once acclimated. Generally advised to feed to satiation only a couple of times per week given the species' slow metabolism. Mammalian feeders such as goldfish are best avoided due to long-term fatty-liver concerns.

goodWith: non-aggressive saltwater fish too large to be swallowed
avoidWith: surgeonfish that may pick at skin algae

imageCandidates.notes: Rickard Zerpe, 4607x3455. Tack-sharp in-situ red morph showing the diagnostic leaf-like dermal appendages and lacy net pattern on a vibrant live specimen; whole fish framed.
Bernard DUPONT, 4416x2649. Sharp lateral yellow-green colour morph on sand, whole animal in frame; adds morph variety and clearly shows frilly appendages.
Steve Childs, 2936x2720. Sharp head-on view of a vibrant red specimen, densely frilled; different angle for variety, full subject in frame.

summary: The Rhinopias Scorpionfish (Rhinopias frondosa) is a cryptic, weed-like ambush predator from the Indo-West Pacific. Venomous spines and a strict live-food preference make it best suited to advanced keepers in a 300+ L species-leaning system.
careNotes:
The Rhinopias Scorpionfish, also sold as the Weedy or Frilly Rhinopias, is a slow-moving ambush predator (family Scorpaenidae) covered in leaf-like dermal appendages — fleshy skin tabs that break up its outline and disguise it among algae, sponges, and coral rubble. It is generally considered an advanced-level fish: highly venomous dorsal, anal, and pelvic spines mean handling with bare hands is strongly discouraged, and stings have been reported to require medical attention. Gloves, tongs, and a barrier in the tank are commonly advised when working inside the system.

Colour variants in the trade include red, yellow, brown, green, and golden morphs — all are the same species. Rhinopias periodically shed an outer cuticle layer, a process often accompanied by cloudy eyes and visible peeling. This is a normal behaviour that helps the fish shed parasites and detritus, not a sign of illness, though excellent water quality is generally advised throughout shed cycles.

Adult specimens reach 20–23 cm and are heavy waste producers, so a tank of around 300 L with a long footprint is generally advised as a practical minimum. The species tends to be peaceful toward similarly-sized or larger tankmates but is unambiguously predatory toward anything small enough to fit in its mouth — small fish, ornamental shrimp, and small crustaceans should be avoided. Corals are generally ignored, but mobile invertebrates are at risk, so this species is considered reef-safe only with caution.

Rhinopias venom is injected via spines and does not disperse into the water column, so tankmates are not at risk of poisoning from the fish's presence. The species is nocturnal to crepuscular and tends to feed best in dim lighting; some keepers report shy behaviour during the day that improves with mature rockwork and limited disturbance.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding has rarely if ever been documented in the aquarium hobby and is generally considered impractical at home-aquarium scale.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Species page for Rhinopias frondosa. Provided: 9 in max, 72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, sg 1.020–1.025, dKH 8–12, 40 gal min (with note 70 gal+ preferable), carnivore, peaceful, moderate care, reef safe with caution.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked: max size 23.0 cm TL, wild temperature 17.5–27.2°C (mean 21°C), depth 10–297 m, Indo-West Pacific distribution (South Africa, southern Japan, Caroline Islands, eastern Australia), family Scorpaenidae, nocturnal predator, IUCN Least Concern. additional.name: ReefApp Encyclopedia
additional.notes: Cross-checked: 23 cm adult size, ~26°C, ~sg 1.026, 100 L minimum, peaceful (predatory to small life), 'delicate / advanced' care, reef safe with caution, periodic skin shedding to shed parasites.

additional.name: Tropical Fish Hobbyist Magazine
additional.notes: Cross-checked: ~9 in max, 20–30 gal suggested (low end of spread, citing low activity), feed to satiation twice weekly, venomous handling warning (gloves/tongs), normal cuticle shedding behaviour, surgeonfish-may-pick-at-skin caution. fish.breedingNotes: Captive breeding is rarely documented and is not considered practical for hobbyists.
